To achieve optimal energy coordination, the proposed methodology incorporates demand response strategies, enabling participants to regulate consumption through controllable loads and storage assets. A structured multi-objective optimization approach formalizes decision-making, embedding the five key User Policies (i.e. economic efficiency, peak power mitigation, sustainability, comfort, and energy efficiency) into an evaluation function that quantifies prosumer preferences. Fairness and arbitrage mechanisms are employed to maintain system equilibrium and prevent inefficiencies in energy trading to achieve the goal of long-term sustainability. The framework dynamically reacts to fluctuations, optimizing energy distribution while maintaining system stability and long-term adaptability. Computationally, the framework leverages genetic optimization techniques to enhance prediction accuracy and decision-making efficiency. The results demonstrate significant improvements in energy allocation, peak demand reduction, and financial optimization, validating the framework's effectiveness. By systematically aligning individual and collective energy objectives, this methodology provides a scalable and adaptive approach to decentralized energy management, reinforcing the resilience and intelligence of next-generation Smart Grids.

title: A Hyperparameter Exploration of LSTM Models for U.S. Airline Tweet Sentiment Analysis
authors: Krish Pillai, Atharva Kawtikwar, Masooda Modak
publish: 2025 1st International Conference on Advancement in Futuristic Technologies (ICAFT)
abstract: This paper examines how deep learning can be used to analyze the reviews of United States airlines on Twitter in terms of sentiment analysis. Although Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks are highly applicable in sequence-based tasks such as text classification, their execution highly relies on the choice of hyperparameters. The paper itself is a systematic experimental research, which aims to measure the influence of major hyperparameters on the accuracy of the model. We search a grid of 18 different model configurations, including an experimentation of three optimizers (Adam, RMSProp, SGD), three dropout rates (0.2, 0.4, 0.6), and two LSTM activation functions (tanh, relu). The models are trained and tested on a dataset consisting of more than 14000 labelled tweets. We find that the choice of optimizer and activation function have a considerable effect on test accuracy, with the Adam and RMSProp optimization functions always better than the SGD and the tanh activation function more effective. The current study offers empirical results that can be used to select the best hyperparameters to use in sentiment analysis of the airline industry.

title: Closed-form High-order Parameter Estimation for Polarimetric Arrays of Arbitrary Configurations
authors: Ruiqian Liao, Fuqian Nie, Hang Zhou, Yaxing Yue
publish: 2025 9th International Conference on Vision, Image and Signal Processing (ICVISP)
abstract: High-order parameter estimation using a polarimetric array benefits from enhanced effective aperture thanks to the exploitation of high-order statistics (HOS). However, when dealing with polarimetric arrays of arbitrary configurations, the estimation procedure faces high computational complexity because the involved parameters, namely direction-of-arrival and polarization, are coupled and require multi-dimensional spectral searching. To address this, we study the closed-form high-order parameter estimation problem for polarimetric arrays by leveraging the polarimetric element space technique. In our proposed approach, we present a general framework to derive the polynomial coefficients involved in the estimation procedure. Thanks to the proposed approach that combines closed-form estimation with HOS, our method achieves low computational complexity while maintaining high effective aperture. Simulation results validate the performance advantages of the proposed approach.

title: Electricity Price Forecasting Using BiLSTM Model with Carbon Market Correlation
authors: Kheleshwar Sahu, Ashish Prajesh, Prerna Jain
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Advanced Research in Computing (ICARC)
abstract: Carbon emissions are mostly caused by the energy industry, particularly by the production of power. Carbon price has become a key tool to deter excessive emissions among the numerous mitigation strategies. With an emphasis on hourly energy price forecasting and carbon cost pass-through, this work examines the connection between electricity market prices and carbon pricing in the Spanish power system. A deep learning framework built on Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory (BiLSTM) networks is employed to capture temporal dependencies in both directions and model long-range relationships in electricity price formation. Correlation research confirms the effectiveness of the model by revealing strong correlations between carbon pricing and actual and predicted electricity costs across Pearson, Spearman, and Kendall Tau metrics. Ordinary Least Squares regression also suggests a carbon cost pass-through of about 2.86 for expected electricity prices and about 2.04 for actual market prices. These findings show how the BiLSTM framework can identify and amplify carbon market signals, providing regulators, policymakers, and market participants with useful information in the context of the ongoing decarbonisation of the power sector.

title: PPDFP: Privacy-Preserving Data Filtering Protocol for Large-Scale ID Alignment of Vertical Federated Learning
authors: Zhengtao Jiang, Hanyu Zhou, Zhengyang Wu, Shujia Li, Yiyang Peng, Chenqing Cai
publish: 2025 13th International Conference on Communications and Broadband Networking (ICCBN)
abstract: Federated learning (FL) enables collaborative model training without raw-data sharing, but its performance hinges on efficient alignment of vertically partitioned samples. Private set intersection (PSI) is the standard tool for this task; however, when sets reach millions or billions of records, the computational and communication burden of PSI becomes prohibitive, especially if the true intersection is sparse. We present the Privacy-Preserving Data Filtering Protocol (PPDFP), a Paillier-homomorphic preprocessing layer that identifies and discards non-overlapping intervals before the heavyweight PSI phase. PPDFP privately determines interval-overlap relations and computes candidate intersection ranges without revealing plaintext endpoints. Experiments on datasets of size 2<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">20</sup>-2<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">24</sup> elements demonstrated that equipping PSI with PPDFP reduced the runtime by 19%-79% and trimmed communication to 0.07%-1.5% of the baseline. These gains empirically demonstrate that PPDFP provides a practical and scalable privacy-preserving preprocessing framework for largescale private set intersection.

title: Day-ahead Scheduling and Reserve Optimization Strategy for Renewable Energy
authors: Peng Yuan, Junjie Sun, Xinwei Li, Dai Cui, Fei Xu, Ling Hao
publish: 2025 4th Asian Conference on Frontiers of Power and Energy (ACFPE)
abstract: The escalating penetration of renewable energy (RE) sources, notably photovoltaic (PV) and wind power, introduces substantial operational complexities for day-ahead scheduling and reserve management in modern power systems, owing to the inherent unpredictability of $\mathbf{R E}$ generation. To address the above challenges, this study proposes a collaborative optimization strategy for day-ahead scheduling and reserve configuration that explicitly accounts for RE uncertainty. The methodology is structured into three sequential stages: first, the probability distribution of RE forecast errors is derived through statistical analysis of historical data, providing a quantitative basis for characterizing uncertainty; second, reserve requirements are systematically determined based on the obtained error distribution to mitigate potential imbalances between supply and demand; and finally, a comprehensive cost-minimization model is developed, incorporating risk costs to hedge against uncertainties. The proposed model rigorously enforces constraints related to power balance, operational limits of thermal units, energy storage systems (ESSs), and grid interactions, while integrating reserve constraints to ensure system stability. Results of the simulations indicate that the proposed day-ahead scheduling framework enables an optimal allocation of reserve capacity, effectively balancing operational security and economic efficiency under uncertain RE conditions.

title: Detecting Test Case Smells Using Prompt Engineering: An Empirical Study in Industry
authors: Lennon Chaves, Flávia Oliveira, Leonardo Tiago
publish: 2025 9th International Conference on Computer, Software and Modeling (ICCSM)
abstract: In the software testing process, test cases are developed to verify whether the software functions as intended. Consequently, test cases must be comprehensible to those who will execute them, and to ensure that they are written comprehensively and accurately, Large Language Models (LLMs) can be employed to assist with this task. With the aim of recommending issues within test cases, this study developed a prompt with 12 smells that should be avoided in test cases to facilitate comprehension. To evaluate the prompt, an empirical study was conducted with 5 participants from a software test team, employing the prompt on test cases from the requirements validation scope of a real industrial scenario. During the study, for each test case, the participants recorded the number of smells recommended by the LLM and subsequently indicated whether they agreed, partially agreed, or disagreed with the response obtained for each test case. Among the results of this experiment, the detection of “Misplaced verification” was observed in 90% of the utilized test cases, while no test case exhibited any “Spell check” smells. Another finding was that approximately 63% of responses from the LLM were classified as “Agree” or “Partially agree” by the participants regarding the smells detected in the test cases, indicating that improvements are necessary for these test cases. Thus, this empirical study presents the results of applying a prompt with 12 test case smells to recommend issues in test cases that validate the requirements of carriers.

title: A Multi-Modal AI Framework for Exotic Fruit Classification and Soil Suitability Mapping Using Deep Learning and Agro-Environmental Data
authors: Rini PR, Kavitha R, Dalvin Vinoth Kumar
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Sentiment Analysis and Deep Learning (ICSADL)
abstract: Rising demand for exotic fruit is challenging accurate identification of crops and evaluation of soil suitability as there are currently no integrated Decision Support Systems (DSS) that incorporate visual and agronomic-environmental information. Methods currently being used for assessing plant potential & determining soil suitability typically address these concerns separately, thereby limiting decision-making accuracy in constructing detailed cultivation plans. This research provides a multi-modal Artificial Intelligence (AI) platform that merges Deep Learning for Images with Machine Learning for soils for the production of exotic fruits. In this framework, Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) are employed to classify images representing both fruit and soil. The Random Forest (RF) algorithm evaluates the potential of a site by utilizing several agronomic and chemical properties of the soil such as pH, Nitrogen, Phosphorus, Potassium (N-P-K) values. This multi-pathway design allows for fruit recommendations or soil recommendations based on either method. With respect to Experimental validation using a self-developed dataset, EfficientNetB0 achieved an accuracy of 93% while MobileNetV2 demonstrated comparable performance (91 %) with the added benefit of having less CPU utilization. The proposed system is a practical, easy-to-use solution for providing guidance to an individual with respect to environmentally friendly exotic fruit farming practices.

title: Motion-Robust Vital Signs Detection Using Millimeter-Wave FMCW Radar Based on DCT-Wavelet Compressed Sensing*
authors: Xin Xiang, Bo Yang, Zehua Nie, Fu Zhang, Kunming Zhou, Ling Chen
publish: 2026 International Conference on Communication Networks and Machine Learning (CNML)
abstract: With the increasing application of millimeter-wave radar technology in non-contact health monitoring, motion interference has become a core challenge limiting its accuracy. This paper proposes an adaptive motion perception and signal reconstruction method based on Frequency Modulated Continuous Wave (FMCW) radar, aiming to achieve robust extraction of respiration and heartbeat signals in dynamic scenarios. The method first utilizes range-dimension Fast Fourier Transform (FFT), Moving Target Indication (MTI), and Constant False Alarm Rate (CA-CFAR) detection to precisely locate the target's chest range bin, effectively overcoming range migration caused by motion. Subsequently, the Extended Differentiate and Cross-Multiply (DACM) algorithm is employed for phase demodulation to avoid phase wrapping distortion. The core contribution lies in the design of a motion detection mechanism based on phase variance. For identified motion interference signals, a Compressed Sensing (CS) reconstruction algorithm combining Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT) and Wavelet bases is proposed. This algorithm effectively suppresses motion noise while accurately reconstructing vital sign signals. Experiments conducted on a platform equipped with a TI IWR6843 radar show that in various motion scenarios, such as walking, the average detection accuracy of respiratory rate and heart rate reached 95.0% and 95.9%, respectively, with Mean Absolute Errors (MAE) of 2.48 BPM and 2.74 BPM. These results verify the robustness and accuracy of the proposed method in dynamic environments, providing reliable technical support for continuous health monitoring.

title: Self - Organizing LoRa Network Based Ocean Observation Platform
authors: Shiqiang Wang, Xuan Geng, Yanli Xu
publish: 2025 13th International Conference on Communications and Broadband Networking (ICCBN)
abstract: At present, the ocean observation platform has the problems of low real-time performance, weak data interaction and poor ad hoc network ability, which cannot meet the observation needs of the marine environment. To adapt to the marine environment, this paper proposes an ocean observation platform system based on STM32 and LoRa network. The marine data collected by the LoRa terminal is transmitted to the gateway, and then transferred to the local core network through the cloud for data processing. The star network topology of multiple LoRa terminals and gateways is realized, which gets rid of using LoRaWAN gateway in the traditional LoRa ad hoc network system. Our solution provides a new concept for offshore observation platforms, which is low-cost, has strong self-organizing network capabilities and offers a wide communication coverage area. Under conditions of low power, short antenna, and close range without obstruction, the overall packet loss rate is only 1.6 %. When there is obstruction, the overall packet loss rate is 5.4%,which has a good performance.

title: EfficientNetB0-based Feature Extraction for Single and Double-Hand Mudra Recognition
authors: Prathviraj Chavan, Pooja Choudhary, Shubhi Upadhyay, Saniya Devarshi, Sumitra Sureliya, Awanit Kumar
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Sentiment Analysis and Deep Learning (ICSADL)
abstract: Bharatanatyam is an ancient classical dance form of India that depicts mudras as a systemic language representing emotions, storytelling, and spiritual expression. Modern learners find it challenging to interpret these correctly due to their visual complexity and minute variations of the hands forming different mudras. We propose a deep-learning-based single-hand and double-hand Bharatanatyam mudra recognition framework using a dataset collected for the purpose. We adopt EfficientNetB0 for feature extraction and classification purposes and implement the deep network using PyTorch. Further, the system is deployed with a modern FastAPI backend and React + TypeScript + Tailwind CSS frontend. Our dataset consists of 51 mudras (28 single-hand, 23 double-hand) with 300 images in each class, which our team has fully collected and annotated. The model obtains an accuracy of 99.71 % and 99.89 % for single- and double-hand mudra recognitions, respectively, which is exceptionally good. This work represents one of the pioneering attempts to develop an automated recognition framework of Bharatanatyam mudras using deep learning, hence contributing to the preservation of culture, digital learning, and performing-arts education.

title: A Hybrid BiLSTM–Transformer–Attention Model for Speech Emotion Recognition
authors: S. M. Ahanaf Tahmid, Ling Lu
publish: 2026 International Conference on Communication Networks and Machine Learning (CNML)
abstract: Speech Emotion Recognition (SER) remains challenging under speaker variability, recording conditions, and multi-dataset evaluation. To capture both local and global dependencies in emotional speech, this paper proposes a hybrid BiLSTM–Transformer–Attention model that integrates bidirectional sequence modeling with multi-head self-attention. Robustness is enhanced through 40-dimensional MFCC features and waveform-level data augmentation (noise injection, time shifting, and time stretching) applied exclusively during training. Experiments on the TESS and RAVDESS datasets use a fixed 80/10/10 train-validation-test split with a random seed of 42. The proposed model achieves 99.91% accuracy on TESS and 92.88% accuracy on RAVDESS, demonstrating consistent performance across datasets with differing levels of variability.

title: Explainable Heart-Disease Risk Prediction Through Boosting-Based Classification and Shap-Guided Clinical Feature Interpretation
authors: S. Vairachilai, Markala Karthik, Sandeep Madarapu
publish: 2026 8th International Conference on Intelligent Sustainable Systems (ICISS)
abstract: Heart disease is one of the major causes of death in the world and it is important that it is predicted at an early stage to enable medical attention. The proposed study suggests an idealized clinical data-informed model, which is comprised of the preprocessing, SMOTE-assisted class balancing, features characteristics selection, and comparative modelling in improving prediction accuracy. Among the entire range of clinical variables, six strongly discriminating features were identified and employed to train Machine Learning and Ensemble Learning models. The findings indicate that the Ensemble Learning performed better than the other methods as AdaBoost had the greatest overall performance of 0.8833 with precision of 1.0000 and ROC-AUC of 0.9621. To guarantee interpretability, SHAP was integrated to make visual the contribution of each feature on the AdaBoost prediction at the individual and global level that would give clinical transparency as opposed to a black-box result. The results prove that the combination of optimized feature subsets, boosting-based learning and SHAP-enabled explainability can provide high predictive power and medical interpretability. The given framework has a high potential of becoming a valid and reliable decision-support tool in terms of early heart-disease risk assessment.

title: Exploring Spatial Redundancy for Boosting the Performance of Stochastic Adcs in Deep Nanometer Cmos
authors: Rodrigo N. Wuerdig, Georges Gielen, Sergio Bampi
publish: 2026 IEEE 17th Latin America Symposium on Circuits and System (LASCAS)
abstract: Employing spatial redundancy allows stochastic ADCs to statistically mitigate non-idealities such as mismatch and noise without relying on precise analog components or calibration. This paper introduces the key principles of stochastic averaging, exploring the boundaries in scaled CMOS processes. We demonstrate that the commonly assumed SNDR improvement of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$10 \cdot \log_{10}(N) dB$</tex> reaches its limits when the quantization noise overwhelms the thermal noise. We highlight that stochastic yields meaningful improvements in the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$F o M_{S}$</tex> only when the power increases sub-linearly with the number of sampling channels. We identify architectural strategies that mitigate the power overhead. Finally, this work outlines key design trade-offs and guides when and how to deploy stochastic principles effectively in deepnanometer ADC architectures.

title: TriCloud: Drug-Target-Disease Ternary Network for Drug Repositioning Research Based on Point Cloud Modeling_supp1-3675301.docx
authors: Wenjun Li
publish: N/A
abstract: In recent years, the “drug-target-disease” association has become increasingly complex and data-scarce. Existing methods are limited by information loss caused by explicit graph construction when modeling triplet relationships, making it difficult to effectively capture geometric structures and long-range dependencies, thereby affecting prediction performance. To address this, this paper proposes a new method based on point cloud modeling—TriCloud. This method represents each triad as a spatial point cloud, encoding semantic and topological relationships through geometric coordinates, and performs feature learning directly on an unordered point set, avoiding reliance on predefined graph structures. Based on the PointNet architecture, it introduces a multi-view feature extraction and fusion mechanism to enhance the modeling capability of global structures and complex spatial patterns. Experimental results show that TriCloud significantly outperforms existing methods on multiple benchmark datasets, achieving an AUC of 0.9995 and an AUPR of 0.9996, with all metrics ranking first. External validation demonstrates its excellent generalization ability. Feature analysis reveals that the geometric-semantic joint features of positive samples play a dominant role in classification. This study provides an efficient and reliable computational framework for drug repurposing, contributing to the development of precision medicine.

title:
VS-ReLEnT:
                    <u>V</u>
                    oltage
                    <u>S</u>
                    caling by
                    <u>R</u>
                    einforcement
                    <u>L</u>
                    earning to Balance
                    <u>E</u>
                    nergy/Reliability
                    <u>T</u>
                    rade-off in STT-MRAM Caches
authors: Alireza Banejad, Amir Mahdi Hosseini Monazzah
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computers
abstract: With the advancement of computing systems, the demand for energy-efficient NVMs has increased due to the recent challenges of traditional memory technologies like SRAMs and DRAMs. Thus, alternative NVMs like STT-MRAM have gained significance. However, one of the significant challenges of STT-MRAMs, is their stochastic/unreliable write operations. To address this, a noticeable amount of energy should be applied to the STT-MRAM cell to perform a reliable write operation. While reliable write operation in STT-MRAMs matter, there are many situations in which the applied write energy is more than enough for the required reliability level. In this research, we proposed VS-ReLEnT to balance the reliability and energy consumption of STT-MRAMs cache write operations. In VS-ReLEnT the write voltage actuation knob of the STT-MRAM-based cache is controlled by a Reinforcement Learning (RL) algorithm. Accordingly, based on the contents that will be written on the STT-MRAM cache block, the suitable voltage is chosen by VS-ReLEnT and applied to the cells. This allows the adjustment of each level of VS-Relent to the most suitable reliability level. The simulation results demonstrate an average energy improvement of 16% compared to the golden case, where all data is written with the highest reliability level while preserving reliability in the system. Conversely, the area overhead and imposed leakage energy of VS-ReLEnT show only a marginal increase of 0.24% and 0.21%, respectively.

title: PrivacyGuard: Differentially Private Machine Learning Against AI-Driven Background Knowledge Attacks
authors: Shoaib Ullah, Razaullah Khan, Madiha Haider Syed, Adeel Anjum
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
abstract: Preserving an individual privacy in the Artificial Intelligence (AI) era is a pressing challenge, as traditional anonymization techniques often fall short against AI-driven threats. While AI has enabled transformative advancements across sectors such as healthcare, finance, aviation, smart cities, and the Internet of Things (IoT), it also introduces new privacy challenges. In particular, AI-driven background knowledge attacks occur when adversaries combine auxiliary information from external sources such as public records, social media, or previously leaked datasets with advanced AI techniques to re-identify individuals or infer sensitive details from anonymized datasets, rendering conventional defenses less effective. In this study, we present PrivacyGuard, an automated framework that integrates machine learning with traditional anonymization to address these risks. PrivacyGuard employs K-Nearest Neighbor and Random Forest classifiers to identify and evaluate privacy risk of each data tuple in AI-driven attack scenarios. Following risk assessment, Laplace-based differential privacy is applied to mitigate identified risks, while fuzzy logic forms equivalence groups that reflect varying risk levels. Experimental validation on the 1:M Adult dataset (an individual having multiple records) demonstrates that PrivacyGuard achieves 96.47% accuracy and mitigates approximately 42.21% of the identified risks, confirming its effectiveness in balancing privacy and data utility against emerging AI-driven threats.

title: Video Respiratory Rate Measurement in Walking Scenarios Using Multi-strategy Adaptive Denoising
authors: Gan Pei, Junhao Ning, Chenrui Niu, Siqiong Yao, Menghan Hu, Guangtao Zhai
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: For non-contact respiratory rate (RR) measurement, effectively addressing the interference from continuous motion artifacts remains a significant challenge. Most existing research focuses on the removal of weak motion artifacts in a two-dimensional plane, and the fixed spatial scale of the scenes limits the generalization of these methods to real-world scenarios, especially in real walking scenarios. To tackle this issue, we propose an RR measurement framework based on a multi-strategy fusion motion artifact suppression algorithm and have constructed a real-world walking dataset. Specifically, the framework consists of three core modules: an ROI automatic selection and adaptive enhancement module to guide the selection of high-quality corner points; a signal quality evaluation module that adaptively assesses whether the signal is noisy, preventing blind denoising; and a multi-strategy fusion motion artifact removal module that dynamically selects the appropriate strategy to suppress motion interference. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first study to investigate the task of video-based RR measurement in real walking scenarios. Experimental results demonstrate that the method achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple datasets, with a mean absolute error (MAE) of 1.04 breaths per minute (bpm) on the COHFACE, 3.17 bpm on the OVRM-Walking dataset, and an average MAE of just 2.41 bpm on the in-house real-world walking dataset, which includes both indoor and outdoor scenarios. This study broadens the applicability of camera-based non-contact RR detection technology. The dataset and code will be publicly available in this project.

title: Adaptive High-Frequency and Temporal Multi-Scale Network for Radar-Based Human Activity Recognition
authors: Peiquan Tian, Hongji Xu, Zhikai Xu, Fei Gao, Hao Zheng, Zihan Ruan, Dongyu Li, Yipeng Xu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: Human activity recognition (HAR) leveraging millimeter-wave (mmWave) radar is a key research area in Internet of Things perception. A key challenge is that existing methodologies often struggle to dynamically identify and preserve high-frequency details in micro-Doppler (MD) spectrograms, which are vital for distinguishing fine-grained activities. Moreover, these approaches lack the ability to capture multi-scale temporal information efficiently and adaptively. To overcome these limitations, the adaptive high-frequency and temporal multi-scale (AHF-TMS) network is proposed. AHF-TMS features a dual-branch parallel architecture that synergistically extracts comprehensive and discriminative features from MD spectrograms. Its spatial branch incorporates the spatial adaptive high-frequency enhancement (SAHFE) module to adaptively enhance critical boundary details. Concurrently, its temporal branch utilizes a multi-scale weighted convolution (MSWConv) module to capture and fuse information from different temporal scales through a weighted strategy. Through comparative experiments on the public datasets IMG848, Ci4R, and RadarMD, the AHF-TMS network achieves accuracies of 96.83%, 93.00%, and 97.84%. Ablation experiments supported by visualizations of convolutional kernels and effective receptive fields validate the effectiveness of the proposed modules. The results confirm that AHF-TMS achieves high recognition accuracy, demonstrates excellent generalization, and is highly effective under data-limited conditions.

title: Lightweight Feature Watermark for Robust Image Attribution in Latent Diffusion Models
authors: Zhangyi Shen, Yuzhong Zhuang, Yani Zhu, Ye Yao
publish: IEEE Signal Processing Letters
abstract: Latent diffusion models (LDMs) have become the dominant paradigm for AI image synthesis, while also posing unprecedented challenges to AI image attribution. To mitigate these risks, digital watermarking has emerged as a crucial technique for embedding traceable identity information into generated content. However, existing watermarking techniques for LDMs often suffer from degraded image quality, limited robustness, and substantial computational and storage overhead. To address these limitations, we propose Feature Watermarking, a novel watermarking paradigm in which pre-trained, randomly sampled feature vectors are leveraged to enable robust attribution capabilities. Subsequently, we employ a dedicated encoder-decoder architecture to generate initial noise and reconstruct features, respectively. Image attribution is performed by measuring the similarity between the original and reconstructed features. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method matches or outperforms existing state-of-the-art approaches in image quality and attribution accuracy, while significantly reducing resource consumption.

title: Wearable sensing technology for knee joint monitoring and functional assessment: a systematic review
authors: Meernah Mohammed Alabdullah, Zhané N. Murrell-Smith, Sheng Quan Xie, Aiqin Liu
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Traditional knee joint monitoring and assessment methods rely heavily on subjective evaluations, leading to an increasing interest in wearable sensing technology for objectively monitoring knee joint function. This review evaluates current research on wearable devices for knee monitoring and functional assessment, highlighting current approaches, limitations, technological advancements and identifying knowledge gaps to guide future research. Following PRISMA guidelines, a systematic search of PubMed, Scopus, IEEE Xplore, Web of Science, Cochrane Library, and Google Scholar identified 77 eligible studies, with methodological quality assessed using the COSMIN Risk of Bias tool. Findings were categorized into four domains: kinetics, kinematics, muscle activity, and integrated domains based on the measured biomechanical parameter. Knee angles were the most frequently measured parameter; however, significant methodological variability was identified, particularly inconsistent sensor placement protocols, calibration methods, and limited real-life applicability. The COSMIN assessment revealed few studies achieved high overall reliability ratings, with kinematics showing the highest consistency and muscle activity the lowest. Kinetic assessments primarily measured knee adduction moments with generally acceptable accuracy but were constrained by indirect measurement methods. Muscle activity assessments predominantly employed surface electromyography, yet exhibited substantial inconsistencies, particularly in electrode placement and analysis techniques. Integrated biomechanical assessments showed significant potential but were constrained by the lack of simultaneous multi-parameter measurement capabilities. Notably, ML/DL integration demonstrated promise, though inconsistencies in performance metrics and sensor configurations complicate direct comparisons, emphasizing the need for standardized evaluation criteria for broader clinical applicability.

title: A Tutorial on Secure and Efficient Firmware Delivery
authors: Dakshina Tharindu, Aruna Jayasena, Prabhat Mishra
publish: IEEE Design & Test
abstract: This tutorial analyzes various aspects of firmware delivery and how they are affected by the introduction of post-quantum safe cryptographic algorithms. Specifically, we describe various firmware delivery stages from both the manufacturer (packaging) and device (deployment) perspectives, including compression (decompression), encryption (decryption), and signing (verification). Extensive experimental evaluation using both real and synthetic benchmarks explores the trade-off between firmware delivery effort (time) and security requirements. Interestingly, our results show that adopting advanced post-quantum digital signature algorithms does not significantly increase firmware deployment time, highlighting their practical viability in next-generation secure firmware delivery pipelines.

title: A First Principles Review of a Novel De Facto Preventive Cybersecurity Strategy
authors: Fazal Raheman, Tejas Bhagat, Panayiotis Kotzanikolaou, Javier G. Meana, Alfredo Cuzzocrea, Vincent Lefebvre, Sandeep Dongre, Anatolly Melnyk, Andrej Grebenc
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Vulnerabilities in ICT infrastructure largely stem from the design practice of granting third-party permissions (TPPs) to software vendors. Our group was the first to demonstrate that TPPs are the root cause of systemic vulnerabilities, and that their elimination could enable a new paradigm—Zero Vulnerability Computing (ZVC). Legacy cybersecurity systems are reactive, multi-step, and human-driven, allowing vulnerabilities to emerge and then attempting to defend against them. This reactive posture contributes to the exponential rise in cybercrime, projected to reach $23.8 trillion by 2027. This paper advances the ZVC paradigm, which was tested and validated in our earlier work on hardware wallet experiments under the European Commission’s Grant program. We further investigate whether eliminating TPPs at the design phase conflicts with the first principles of software engineering. Rather than revalidating efficacy, we adopt a hypothesis-building approach supported by the historical deconstruction of computer design rules and previous empirical validation. Our analysis reveals that TPPs are recurrent design constructs rather than axiomatic necessities, and that their removal is architecturally viable. We evaluate ZVC’s viability and offer a comparative analysis that distinguishes ZVC from legacy Zero Trust models. ZVC’s core protocol operates without traditional user-facing cryptography, making it inherently resistant to quantum computing threats and capable of addressing approximately 90% of known “structural vulnerabilities.” The remaining 10%, mostly “semantic vulnerabilities,” can be mitigated through proactive strategies integrated with artificial intelligence. This review lays the foundation for a preventive, quantum-resilient cybersecurity paradigm that is automated, cost-effective, and future-proof. We conclude by outlining implementation challenges, open research questions, and future directions.

title: A Scenario-Driven AI+VR Language Practice Platform: A Technical Feasibility and Formative Usability Study
authors: Zeynep Cipiloglu Yildiz, Furkan Baytak, Furkan Ozkaya
publish: IEEE Transactions on Learning Technologies
abstract: We present a scenario-driven AI+VR language practice platform designed to support immersive role-play and open-ended spoken interaction for L2 speaking practice. The system combines (i) interactive VR scenarios, (ii) LLM-driven real-time conversations, (iii) a block-based scenario authoring tool for customizing practice content, (iv) reflective analytics derived from conversation logs, and (v) a privacy-aware social layer for peer support. The platform integrates the OpenAI GPT-4 Realtime API within the Unity 3D engine to enable low-latency conversational interaction in immersive environments. We report a technical feasibility assessment and a formative usability evaluation. In a study with 30 learners, the platform achieved a mean System Usability Scale (SUS) score of 73.25, a p95 frame time of 10.69 ms, and an average AI response latency of 0.77 s. These results provide initial evidence that the proposed design can deliver responsive AI-mediated dialogue and a usable end-to-end experience for scenario-based practice. We conclude with design trade-offs, key limitations of the current short-term evaluation, and directions for learning-oriented and longitudinal studies.

title: Battery State-of-Health Estimation across Diverse Aging Conditions with Intra-Inter Cycle Fusion Perception
authors: Yuan Cao, Xinzhi Zhang, Yinqin Liao, Bingchuan Wang, Chunsheng Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Transportation Electrification
abstract: Accurate State-of-Health (SOH) estimation is paramount for the safe and reliable operation of lithium-ion batteries. However, conventional methods often overlook inter-cycle degradation dynamics. Furthermore, existing predictive models, primarily developed and validated within a restricted set of aging conditions and battery types, raise doubts regarding their extensive applicability. Therefore, the I²C-transformer is proposed—a deep learning framework specifically designed for battery SOH estimation across diverse aging conditions. Its feature extraction component comprises a unique I²C-convolutional module. Based on an analysis of how inter-cycle relaxation voltage (RV) variations impact SOH, this module employs fast fourier transform (FFT) to adaptively identify critical historical cycles. Subsequently, it fuses intra- and inter-cycle RV data via a 1-dimensional convolutional neural network (1D-CNN) to extract comprehensive aging characteristics. These features are fed into a lightweight transformer to establish a mapping relationship with SOH, an architecture that innovatively dispenses with the traditional positional encoding module. The model achieved a mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) of 0.75% across four large-scale datasets encompassing various aging conditions, battery chemistries, and initial SOCs of relaxation. Additionally, transfer experiments demonstrated its strong generalization capability and practical applicability in diverse real-world scenarios. Finally, we deployed our model on an embedded platform, where each inference takes only 0.65 milliseconds to complete. We expect this study to promote exploration of cross-cycle insights for SOH estimation and facilitate battery research across comprehensive aging factors.

title: Modeling Driver Lateral Control Behavior for Human–Machine Shared Takeover During Emergency Collision Avoidance Under Dynamic Traffic Environment
authors: Shiyong Feng, Yafei Wang, Chen Zhou, Zheng Wang, Yan Han, Feixiang Xu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: Understanding and modeling driver’s lateral control behavior accurately are essential for developing effective human–machine shared takeover (HMST) system during emergency collision avoidance under dynamic traffic environment. However, little attention has been devoted to modeling driver behavior during HMST. Moreover, existing methods mostly focus on the interaction between a single vehicle and the driver, making it difficult to effectively characterize the nonlinear and uncertain behavior of drivers in dynamic traffic environment. Therefore, this study focuses on modeling of driver lateral control behavior during HMST under dynamic traffic environment, and a Transformer-based modeling method with multi-head attention is proposed. To validate the performance of the purposed approach, comparative experiments against traditional modeling approaches are carried out. And twelve drivers are recruited to perform HMST tasks in different emergency collision avoidance scenarios based on a driving simulator. Results of comparative experiments of different driving scenarios demonstrate the superior accuracy of the proposed method. In addition, the shapley (SHAP) additive explanations is employed to enhance the interpretability of the proposed model, thereby providing a reliable foundation for the development of efficient HMST systems.

title: IEEE Computing Edge
authors: N/A
publish: Computer

title: Velocity Estimation in ISAC Systems with Non-uniform Sensing Resource based on 5G NR Frame Structure
authors: Boshi Wang, Songtao Gao, Cunhua Pan, Hong Ren, Zhiyuan Yu, Yiming Yu, Jiangzhou Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology
abstract: Integrated Sensing and Communication (ISAC) is a promising technology that improves resource utilization efficiency and reduces costs by sharing physical hardware and spectrum. However, in practical fifth-generation (5G) New Radio (NR) frame structures, different time slots may include various auxiliary signals for synchronization, switching, or calibration, resulting in non-uniform intervals between sensing signals. In this paper, we firstly consider a Time Division (TD)-ISAC system model and examine the Cram´ er-Rao Lower Bound (CRLB) for velocity estimation with arbitrary resource spacing. Interestingly, the derived CRLB reveals that non-uniformly distributed sensing resources are not only necessary but are also beneficial. Nonetheless, velocity estimation using non-uniformly distributed sensing resources often leads to high Doppler sidelobes when employing traditional Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)-based methods. To address this issue, we propose a velocity estimation algorithm that incorporates a dictionary-based coarse estimation and phase unwrapping for refinement. By further adopting the RELAX (Relaxation) framework, the method can not only identify multiple targets but also mitigate the impact of static clutter. Finally, simulation results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed algorithm, which achieves higher estimation precision and lower computational complexity than the existing methods, even with a limited number of grid points.

title: Adaptive Parameter Tuning for Fused Localization Model Using Multi-agent Reinforcement Learning
authors: Wan-Ning He, Xin-Lin Huang, Shengyu Wang, Zhongwei Xu, Cheng Li, Shui Yu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology
abstract: In fully automatic operation of railways, it is taken as a promising solution of high-precise localization to fuse ultra wide band (UWB) and inertial measurement unit (IMU). However, the inherent errors caused by non-line of sight (NLoS) propagation and IMU cumulative errors are inevitable, which always leads to similar localization errors along the same track. Different from existing localization schemes by spatio-temporal correlations from one train, we propose data mining scheme among different trains from the view point of multi-agent reinforcement learning (RL). Firstly, UWB ranging data filtering mechanism is modeled as a deep Q-network (DQN), which can kick out ranging data under serious NLoS interferences. Secondly, we propose a parameter tuning scheme for fused localization model to update adaptively, which integrates data among multi train along the same track. Finally, semi-supervised rewards are evaluated to facilitate DQN training and minimize cumulative localization errors, with the help of unit point locators. Simulation results show that proposed scheme outperforms other three typical fused schemes, with gains of 11.59%, 9.74%, 4.43% in LoS scenarios and 22.13%, 13.77%, 14.77% in NLoS scenarios.

title: Conference Committee: ICCSM 2025
authors: N/A
publish: 2025 9th International Conference on Computer, Software and Modeling (ICCSM)

title: Design of Sports Tourism Demand Prediction System Based on Big Data and Machine Learning Algorithms
authors: Jun Zhang
publish: 2025 International Conference on Power, Electrical Engineering, Electronics and Control (PEEEC)
abstract: There is a remarkable growth in the application and use of machine learning in tourism. This allows for an organized proposal of a predictive system. The research work in this paper focuses on the development of a sports tourism demand prediction system using big data and machine learning. It is aimed at processing the big data about sports tourism, such as historical tourism data, demographic data, climate conditions, and calendar of events to forecast future demand. Using such techniques as decision trees, random forests, and neural networks, one is able to identify various factors that affect tourism demand. Big data integration improves the system's predictive capability since it captures detailed information about different factors influencing tourism behaviours. The results of the experiment prove that the system is effective in predicting demand and can be useful for tourism operators. This work will help to allocate resources more effectively and plan further development in the sphere of sports tourism, which will enhance its efficiency and sustainability.

title: AURA- Autism Understanding and Rehabilitation Assistant
authors: Renju Rachel Varghese, Anagha Devi P, Hima Rose George, Aleesha Mariam Sabu, Ann Mariya Jose K
publish: 2025 International Conference on Emerging Trends in Signal Processing &amp; Computational Intelligence (ICCSPCI)
abstract: Early detection of neurodevelopmental disorders is critical for ensuring timely interventions and improving long-term outcomes. Among these, Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) presents unique diagnostic challenges, as traditional assessments are subjective, resource-intensive, and often delayed. To address this, we explore machine learning (ML) techniques for automated ASD prediction using behavioral and clinical datasets. Three models were implemented and compared: Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost), Linear Discriminant Analysis with AdaBoost, and a Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) neural network. Although XGBoost achieved 100% accuracy on training data, it showed limited generalization, highlighting overfitting. The MLP demonstrated superior reliability, achieving 99.53% accuracy with a 0.9966 F1-score, making it the most effective model. The LDA+AdaBoost pipeline also produced competitive results with 94.31% accuracy. These findings emphasize the robustness of deep learning, particularly MLP, in ASD detection. The proposed framework can assist clinicians in reliable early screening, thereby supporting timely diagnosis and effective management.

title: Hybrid Computational Techniques for Evaluating Biofuel Blends: Impact of Nano Additives on Internal Combustion Engines
authors: Ankita Thakur, Malepati Chandra Sekhar, Prem Nath Suman
publish: 2025 15th International Conference on Mathematics, Actuarial Science, Computer Science and Statistics (MACS)
abstract: The increasing demand for sustainable energy sources has prompted extensive research into biofuels as viable alternatives to traditional petroleum-based fuels. This research investigates the impact of nano-additives on the emissions and performance of biofuel blends in Internal Combustion Engines (ICEs). Specifically, the research focuses on evaluating biofuels derived from Eichhornia crassipes (water hyacinth) and their blends with nano-additives, such as metal oxides, to enhance engine performance. Mamdani Fuzzy Inference Adaptive Gravitational Search Optimization (MFI-AGSO) is employed to predict the exhaust emissions and performance characteristics of these fuel blends. Adaptive Gravitational Search Optimization (AGSO) is used to fine-tune its parameters and select the best operating conditions for lower emissions and improved performance. Mamdani Fuzzy Inference system (MFIS) is applied to achieve more accurate predictions. Key input parameters such as Engine Load (EL), fuel blend percentage, nanoparticle concentration, and Injection Pressure (IP) are analyzed to determine their effects on outputs such as Brake Thermal Efficiency (BTE), Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC), and exhaust emissions. The system is implemented using Python software, and the MFI-AGSO model outperformed conventional methods in predicting emissions characteristics and engine performance of biofuel blends. With the B25Ce50 blend, the BTE is 33.4%, the BSFC is <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.255 \text{kg} / \text{kWh}$</tex>, the Carbon Monoxide (CO) emissions achieve 0.234%, the Nitrogen-oxides (NOx) emissions are 595 parts per million (ppm), and the HC emissions are 36 ppm. The results indicate that the optimal biofuel blend with nano additives improves performance and also reduces harmful emissions compared to conventional fuels. This research provides valuable insights into the potential of biofuels with nano additives as an eco-friendly and efficient alternative for ICEs.

title: An Optimized Machine Learning Framework for Heart Disease Detection Using the Jellyfish Algorithm
authors: Babby E, K. Rajakumari, Karthik V
publish: 2025 15th International Conference on Mathematics, Actuarial Science, Computer Science and Statistics (MACS)
abstract: We are seeing millions of people dying from heart problems every year only, making it one of the most common and dangerous diseases in the world. Early and correct identification actually helps reduce death rates and definitely improves patient outcomes. Machine learning itself provides strong tools for disease prediction due to its speed, costeffectiveness, and accuracy. This study further proposes a hybrid approach combining Jellyfish Optimization Algorithm with Artificial Neural Network and Decision Tree classifiers for heart disease prediction. Basically, JOA was used to reduce the dimensions of the Cleveland Heart Disease dataset to solve the curse of dimensionality problem and improve generalization. The same JOA method quickly found the best feature subsets and greatly improved model performance. As per the improved dataset, the ANN and DT classifiers were trained regarding the classification process. The ANN model achieved 97% accuracy, which was slightly better than the DT model itself that showed 96% accuracy. Further analysis revealed that ANN performed marginally superior to DT. Research shows that JOA actually works well to make predictions better and select good features. When doctors use Jellyfish Optimization with simple machine learning models like ANN and DT, they can definitely build reliable systems that help detect heart disease early and make better treatment decisions.

title: Revolutionizing Microcontroller Virtualization: Exploring Micro-Controller as a Service (MaaS) on AWS
authors: Vijay Savani, Palak Kapuriya, Tapan Patel, Jitendra Bhatia, Malaram Kumhar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Emerging Trends and Innovations in ICT (ICEI)
abstract: Microcontroller-as-a-Service (MaaS) is an emerging model that applies cloud principles such as scalability, resource pooling, and remote access to physical embedded hardware. Microcontrollers are increasingly becoming central components of IoT and embedded systems education; due to their limited availability and cost, though, logistical concerns impede practical learning processes. MaaS allows students to remotely execute programs on real hardware that resides in the cloud. This paper describes the proof-of-concept implementation of MaaS with Amazon Web Services. It explain the architecture, system components, and automation workflow that enable uploading MicroPython programs by users for execution on a Raspberry Pi Pico board and fetching outputs through cloud services. We show real interaction logs (SCP/SSH/EC2) that demonstrate the operational feasibility of MaaS. It further discuss issues of scalability, security implications, comparison with state-of-theart remote lab tools, and directions for future expansion and empirical evaluation.

title: mosaiks are made of tesserae: GUI design for a co-simulation framework
authors: Eike Schulte, Jan Sören Schwarz, Malte Stomberg, Sharaf Alsharif, Danila Valko, Jirapa Kamsamsong
publish: 2026 Open Source Modelling and Simulation of Energy Systems (OSMSES)
abstract: In a mosaic, a tessera is a single stone. We introduce tesserae for the co-simulation framework mosaik, where they are sets of entities. They allow for a visual, intuitive, and yet systematic description of simulation scenarios by allowing their entities to be created together and the entities of two tesserae to be connected simultaneously, while ensuring that multidirectional data-flow between tesserae remains consistent without further manual synchronization. We further present an extension of mosaik by a graphical user interface (GUI) based on these tesserae, enabling the drag-and-drop creation of co-simulation setups and their execution. The GUI aims to make mosaik more accessible to users previously excluded by its script-based nature. At the same time, it preserves mosaik’s flexibility, extensibility, and modular architecture.

title: Nature Inspired Hierarchical Optimization for Predictive Load Balancing in Multi-Cloud Environments
authors: Shalini A, Mridulla S, R Mohandas, Kandavel N, M Vinodhini, Karthikeyan P
publish: 2026 8th International Conference on Intelligent Sustainable Systems (ICISS)
abstract: Multi-cloud computing helps companies to use services from multiple cloud providers, improving reliability and cost optimization, but efficiently balancing workloads across different clouds is still a major problem. This paper introduces a simple and effective hierarchical optimization approach for load balancing in multi cloud environments by combining three nature inspired algorithms Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO), Ant Colony Optimization (ACO), and Artificial Bee Colony (ABC) with the LSTM prediction model. In this method, PSO is used to select the best cloud provider, ACO allocates tasks to suitable data centers and ABC distributes tasks among virtual machines. In order to support proactive resources allocation, the future workload is predicted by the LSTM model. Experiments conducted using the Google Cluster Trace shows that the proposed Hierarchical Optimization Algorithm (HOA) accomplishes 96% improvement in multi objective optimization compared to traditional methods. This ensures a robust solution for heterogeneous multi cloud load balancing and a smooth failover between cloud providers during service disruptions.

title: Survey of malware classification Models for Multi-source data
authors: Lu Hou, Chao Li, Ruliang Lin, Xintong Lin, Mengyu Xia, Yiming Shi
publish: 2026 International Conference on Communication Networks and Machine Learning (CNML)
abstract: With the continuous evolution of network security threats, the detection and classification of malicious code has become a core topic in the field of information security. The malicious code classification model based on multi-source data can effectively improve the detection accuracy and model robustness by fusing multi-modal information such as static features, dynamic behavior features and network traffic. This paper systematically reviews the research status of malware classification models driven by multi-source data, deeply analyzes the main pain points faced by current models, and summarizes the key technologies such as feature extraction methods, data preprocessing processes and model architecture design. On this basis, this paper further discusses possible future research directions, including the development of multimodal deep fusion methods, the improvement of model robustness and interpretability, and the construction of high-quality malicious code data resources. This paper aims to provide a reference for the research of malicious code classification and promote the application of related technologies in the field of network security.

title: Automated Waste Sorting Based on MobileNetV3 with Transfer Learning
authors: Chenan Wu, Yanning Yang, Xingxing Yang, Jingyi Shi
publish: 2025 International Conference on Computing, Robotics and System Sciences (ICRSS)
abstract: Traditional waste sorting methods primarily rely on manual operations, presenting limitations of high costs and low efficiency. In contrast, automated waste sorting algorithms based on deep learning can significantly reduce manual labor intensity. The MobileNetV3 model adopted in this study, combined with transfer learning, significantly reduces the number of parameters while markedly improving computational efficiency. To validate the effectiveness of the proposed method, comparative experiments were conducted with mainstream models including MobileNetV2, DenseNet121, ResNet18, and GoogleNet after confirming the feasibility of transfer learning. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed MobileNetV3 model achieves the optimal classification accuracy of 0.9123.

title: Design of Performance Centric Multiplier Architecture with Dynamic Adder Selection for Digital Signal Processing Applications
authors: V. Sanjay, S.Kavi Shaghar, C.K. Balasundari, S.Sankara Kuttralam
publish: 2026 8th International Conference on Intelligent Sustainable Systems (ICISS)
abstract: In Digital Signal Processing (DSP), Computer Arithmetic, and VLSI System Design, fast multiplication is a key requirement. Traditional array multipliers typically have delay, area or power issues related to the many partial products being created, and the complexity of the addition. In order to reduce the number of partial products, this work proposes a modification to an 8-bit multiplier based on Radix-4 Booth encoding method which will condense the original eight partial products down to four by grouping the multiplier bits into segments of three-bits. To help eliminate the partial product matrix some exact 3: 2 compressors are integrated into the partial product reduction. For the last accumulation stage, we also utilize a configurable adder, to combine the sum row and the shifted carry row, to address the row addition. Each specific design for the adder architecture, Ripple Carry Adder (RCA), Conditional Sum Adder (CSA), Carry Save Adder (CSA), and Kogge-Stone Adder (KSA), can be independently enabled using the existing decoding control logic architecture to create the structure suitable for the parameters of the design. The results of the study showed that the Radix-4 Booth-based multiplier contributes to improved results in speed, power efficiency, and area effectiveness than traditional designs. The flexibility of this architecture makes it very suitable for real-time mathematical and signal processing, where high performance is required, but needs low power consumption. From FPGA device family Virtex Ultrascale+: xcu250-figd2104-2L-e, the obtained implementation results shows that the design's efficient performance utilizes 15 CLBs and 102 LUTs with 24.139 W of dynamic power and 3.541 W of static power, totaling an on-chip power of 27.68 W. The design can achieve a data path delay of 7.37 ns with 10 logic levels for a PDP of 204.002. The findings illustrate that the architecture has a very high performance-topower ratio, which would be ideal for high-speed and low-power VLSI applications.

title: Data-Driven Crop Yield Forecasting in Tamil Nadu Using Advanced Deep Learning Models
authors: A. Philip C Jacob, B. D. Pamela, C. P. KingstonStanley, D. D. Bini
publish: 2026 2nd International Conference on Advances in Intelligent Computing and Applications (AICAPS)
abstract: The investigation presents the development and evaluates a forecasting framework which uses deep learning to make agricultural predictions through a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network. The proposed framework addresses the limitations of conventional statistical forecasting approaches which struggle to represent the complex and nonlinear characteristics of agricultural time-series data during their projections. The data shows strong susceptibility to changes brought about by climatic variations and seasonal agricultural practices and environmental transformations. The model achieves accurate agricultural forecasting results because it uses LSTM networks to learn both long-term dependencies and short-term pattern changes. The agricultural datasets from each district underwent complete pre-processing procedures which improved model performance through subsequent tuning of critical hyper parameters. The proposed frame achieved strong predictive performance because its $\mathbf{R}^{\mathbf{2}}$ values went beyond 0.90 while it maintained low RMSE and MAE results throughout the training and testing periods. The residual diagnostics showed minimal bias because the mean errors stayed close to zero and the skewness remained low which confirmed the predictions had stable and trustworthy results. The validation metrics maintained their stability throughout the training process which proved the model could generalize well without showing any signs of overfitting. The model demonstrates its potential as a dependable decision-support system for Tamil Nadu agricultural planning based on resource management and supply chain optimization and evidence-based policy development. The research uses LSTM to model residual analysis which includes agro-climatic factors as its main focus.

title: Adaptive SoC-Dependent Fast Charging Optimisation with Degradation-Aware Scheduling in DC Microgrid Architecture for Electric Vehicles
authors: Vikram Kumar Saxena, Kundan Kumar, Benjamin A Shimray, Sanjeet Kr. Dwivedi
publish: 2025 IEEE 12th Uttar Pradesh Section International Conference on Electrical, Electronics and Computer Engineering (UPCON)
abstract: The rapid growth of electric vehicles (EVs) demands fast-charging systems that ensure both user convenience and battery health while minimizing grid stress. Conventional fast-charging methods often ignore the nonlinear state-of-charge (SoC)-dependent charging behaviour of lithiumion batteries, leading to accelerated degradation and grid instability. This paper proposes an adaptive fast-charging framework for DC microgrid-based EV charging stations that integrates SoC-dependent nonlinear charging power constraints with semi-empirical battery degradation models combining both calendar and cycling ageing effects. A real-time model predictive control (MPC)-based scheduling algorithm dynamically adjusts charging power and energy allocation from the grid, photovoltaic generation, and battery energy storage system (BESS) to balance charging speed, degradation cost, and grid load. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed framework reduces battery degradation by over 60%, lowers grid peak demand by 35%, and decreases total grid energy consumption by 19% compared to conventional fast charging. The proposed approach offers a scalable, health-preserving, and grid-friendly solution for next-generation EV fast charging infrastructure.

title: Automated Bone Fracture Detection using X-Ray Images with CNN
authors: M. Jagadish, M. Akash, N. Kathirvel
publish: 2026 8th International Conference on Intelligent Sustainable Systems (ICISS)
abstract: Major cause of worldwide emergency visits is as a result of bone fractures. Radiographs interpreted by hand are tedious and subject to errors, especially minor fractures. The hypothesis is the hybrid deep learning framework based on the unique detection system, with the option of automation. This model combines two or more sophisticated overarches to preserve the global context and tight-scale fracture structure and does not require any feature engineering. The system has been evaluated on a multi-body part, thoroughly annotated Kaggle dataset of radiographs, and uses multi-stage ductus avoidance through thorough preprocessing and augmentation under adverse conditions of imaging variation. It has a classification accuracy of 99.8 that is much higher than traditional and standard deep learning methods. This shows a high level of accuracy of the framework and the capability of scaffolding the radiologists as well as speed up diagnosis and efficiency of clinical workflow.

title: Optimized Architecture and Strategies for High Performance Computing in Cloud and Hybrid Environments
authors: S. Shanthi, R. Karthik, R. Madhuramya, Vimit Varghese, G.M.Banu Priya, S. Ramasamy
publish: 2026 8th International Conference on Intelligent Sustainable Systems (ICISS)
abstract: High-performance computing has traditionally been based on large on-site supercomputers that provide powerful but costly and rigid performance. HPC workloads may now benefit from flexible on-demand access to computing resources because to the expansion of cloud computing. Yet issues like inconsistent performance slow networks and complex data that processing persist when HPC is used to cloud or hybrid environments. A practical method for enhancing the performance of HPC applications in the cloud and hybrid settings is presented in this study. The framework leverages hardware that the acceleration and intelligent scheduling for containerized programs to balance the cost of performance and resource use. To lower communication latency, it also uses the software-defined networking adaptive task transfer and data-aware scheduling. When compared to conventional cloud-based HPC systems that experimental testing using the scientific and industrial workloads demonstrates up to 17% quicker performance. An average 10- 14% reduction in cloud compute cost, primarily due to reduced billed execution time on cloud resources. The results demonstrate that while retaining scalability to security and sustainability the suggested hybrid HPC architecture can manage the demanding applications such as genetic research, climate modeling, and AI simulations.

title: An Efficient Deepfake Image Detection Framework Based on Facial Landmark Localization and Deep Feature Extraction
authors: Aryan Upadhyay, Sujay Pandey, Utkarsh Kumar Pandey, Priyanshu Varshney, Shelly Gupta, Mukesh Kumar Tripathi
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Sentiment Analysis and Deep Learning (ICSADL)
abstract: Recent developments in Deep Learning have made it frighteningly easy to create artificially realistic content, resulting in an increasingly depressing reality crisis on all digital media. These are systems that have been extracted from the lab and that have acquired social, political and other public uses in which they often are being misused, as in the case of fraud, disinformation and identity theft. The increasing risk of this threat further underscores the need for effective and automatic deepfake detection systems. To counter this threat, author herein present Deep Shield as a generic framework to recognise the AI-generated visual content. The system employs an MTCNN for precise face detection and alignment, and the classifier is trained to focus on facial regions of interest where artefacts are likely to occur. The extracted facial features are processed by a Meso-Net-based architecture designed to capture minor mesoscopic distortions and pixel-level warping artefacts arising from GAN-based synthesis. Unlike typical forensics techniques that get nullified under heavy compression, Deep Shield employs special preprocessing to preserve information at high frequencies. With training on a cross-dataset set based on Face Forensics++, the method shows strong generalisation to various resolutions and compression factors. Experimental results suggest that Deep Shield is capable of achieving better accuracy and efficiency than traditional classifiers, indicating the practicability and scalability of Deep Shield to real-time content verification and digital forensics.

title: PC-MAL: Enhancing Log Labeling Consistency via Multi-Agent Collaboration and Reflective Feedback
authors: Jiarui Liu, Yang Ji
publish: 2026 International Conference on Communication Networks and Machine Learning (CNML)
abstract: In the operation and maintenance of complex software systems, log analysis is crucial for anomaly detection and fault diagnosis. However, manual log labeling is labor-intensive and error-prone due to diverse log formats and complex semantics. While Large Language Models (LLMs) show promise for automated labeling, single-agent approaches are often sensitive to prompt design and may suffer from semantic drift (e.g., confusing recovery messages with failures), leading to unstable results. To address these challenges, this paper proposes PC-MAL, a multi-agent labeling mechanism that combines Prompt Collaboration with Reflective Feedback. PC-MAL adopts a closed-loop process to improve consistency and accuracy under a comparable token budget. The framework consists of four agents: Task Decomposer, Annotator Agents, Reviewer, and Prompt Optimizer. Experiments on the public LogHub dataset show that PC-MAL achieves 91.0% accuracy and a Fleiss’ Kappa of 0.87 on the Zookeeper test set, outperforming strong baselines such as self-consistency and debate-style multi-agent methods. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of PC-MAL for structured log labeling tasks.

title: Transfer Learning for Remaining Useful Life Prediction with Irregular Time-Series under Small-Sample and Right-Censored Conditions: An Application to Avionics Systems
authors: Jinhe Yu, Jianyin Zhao
publish: 2026 International Conference on Communication Networks and Machine Learning (CNML)
abstract: Accurate remaining useful life (RUL) prediction is crucial for the health management of avionics systems, yet it is often hindered by irregularly sampled time-series data, limited labeled samples, and right-censored degradation trajectories. These challenges substantially reduce the effectiveness of conventional data-driven prognostic methods. This paper proposes a transfer learning–based framework for RUL prediction under small-sample and right-censored conditions. Knowledge from data-rich source domains is transferred to target avionics systems with scarce failure data to enhance model generalization. A time-aware representation learning strategy is employed to model irregular temporal intervals without uniform resampling. In addition, a censoring-aware learning mechanism is incorporated to effectively utilize right-censored observations during training. Experimental results on avionics system datasets show that the proposed approach outperforms representative baseline methods in prediction accuracy and robustness, particularly in small-sample scenarios. The results demonstrate the practicality of transfer learning for RUL prediction with irregular and censored time-series data in avionics applications.

title: Spatially-suppressed Reliability-gated Memory for Transformer Tracking
authors: Aoshen Hao
publish: 2026 International Conference on Communication Networks and Machine Learning (CNML)
abstract: Single-stream Transformer trackers have achieved remarkable success by unifying feature extraction and relation modeling. However, many existing methods still suffer from model drift in long-term scenarios, largely due to blind online updates where unreliable observations (e.g., occlusions and dis-tractors) are inadvertently written into historical representations. In this work, we propose SRMTrack, a reliability-gated one-stream tracking framework that maintains a compact set of high-quality Appearance Tokens as historical cues. Unlike complex multi-branch designs, SRMTrack integrates historical information through a unified Appearance Decoder with lightweight cross-attention. Crucially, we introduce a Reliability Assessment module that is executed after generating the current prediction (score map and bounding box) to decide whether the current frame should be used for updating the Appearance Tokens. This assessment employs two rigorous gates: (1) a Low-Confidence Filter that rejects updates when the target is likely lost and heavily occluded; and (2) a Multi-Peak Filter with Spatial Suppression that masks the primary peak region to accurately detect potential distractors and skip ambiguous updates. During inference, the curated Appearance Tokens are fused with the current search features via the Appearance Decoder, improving robustness while avoiding error accumulation. Experiments on standard benchmarks demonstrate that SRMTrack effectively mitigates memory corruption and consistently improves robustness in complex scenarios.

title: Enhancing Organizational Security through Specialized Deep Transfer Learning for Threat Identification
authors: Vijayakumar G, Nelson S, Mohan Dhass G, Mohamed Ashraf M, Logesh M, Madhankumar M
publish: 2025 1st International Conference on Advancement in Futuristic Technologies (ICAFT)
abstract: Organizations should run advanced security methods to proactively detect and counter combating against cyber threats. Deep Transfer Learning (DTL) has emerged as a powerful technique for leveraging the application of pre-trained deep learning models against the domain of cyber security. This study introduces a DTL framework that integrates domain knowledge and adaptive feature learning to enhance threat detection accuracy, meaning this model will transfer its knowledge from large-scale security datasets to detect newer threats with almost zero labelled data. Unlike conventional machine learning, fine-tuned deep neural networks capture attack signatures across vectors. The experiments reported in this paper prove that DTL has certainly given a better scenario in hunting down malware, phishing, and intrusions, as it reduced false alarms while offering quite a reliable defensive measure. Because of its ultimate ability to learn adaptively, the framework behaves on its own by means of self-improvement and is more scalable against new threats. Here is a clear emphasis on DTL's ability to pave the way for the emergence of intelligent, automated, and adaptive security systems, hastening the pace to fight against cyber dangers while buttressing enterprise safety.

title: AI Driven Cultural Preservation Multimodal Language Models for Intangible Heritage Image Editing
authors: Taorui Huang
publish: 2026 International Conference on Communication Networks and Machine Learning (CNML)
abstract: This study looks into new ways to edit images and videos of intangible cultural heritage using simple language models. The goal is to help save tangible cultural heritage with AI. The researchers combined computer vision, natural language processing, and deep learning to create an AI system. This system can understand cultural backgrounds and make smart image edits. It can spot and protect cultural details in images, while also letting users make creative changes using plain language. Tests showed that the new method, called CARE-MLLM, makes editing intangible cultural heritage images faster and better, while keeping the culture true. The study also talks about ethical issues and what could happen next in AI-based cultural preservation. Overall, this research offers a fresh tech approach to protecting intangible cultural heritage in the digital age, which is of great significance for promoting cultural diversity and heritage preservation in human society.

title: Advanced AI Systems for Noise Suppression in Wireless Signal Transmission Using Deep Learning
authors: M. Jyothi, R. Senthamarai, Narasimhulu K, Gotte Ranjith Kumar, Gangu Rama Naidu, M. Nathiya
publish: 2025 1st International Conference on Advancement in Futuristic Technologies (ICAFT)
abstract: Modern wireless communication systems encounter significant performance deterioration due to noise sources that decrease their reliability criteria. The document shows how deep learning algorithms enabled an improved AI-controlled system for reducing wireless signal transmission noise. The study analyses the combining power of CNNs and LSTM networks to remove time-series wireless signal noise. Both CNNs and LSTMs operate differently in signal processing through feature extraction because they detect spatial patterns in noisy data, but LSTM specifically retains long temporal dependencies to support dynamic noise suppression mechanisms. The proposed architectural structure selects CNNs to obtain local features along with LSTM networks which detect sequence patterns because noise manifests temporally. The CNN-LSTM model produces outstanding results for noise reduction applications by achieving better SNR levels and BER reduction based on experimental testing. Proof from this research indicates deep learning methods demonstrate robust potential to develop signal noise reduction solutions and deploy them directly in communication system noise cancellation.

title: Dynamic Optimization Model of Financial Transaction Key Management Based on Quantum Random Number Generator
authors: Ting Zhu, Lingfei Peng
publish: 2025 International Conference on Information Technology, Communication Ecosystem and Management (ITCEM)
abstract: In view of the background that financial transactions are facing the threat of quantum computing, this paper proposes a dynamic optimization model of key management based on quantum random number generator (QRNG), aiming to solve the contradiction between security and efficiency of traditional encryption systems through quantum enhancement technology. Methodologically, this paper integrates a four-layer architecture, the quantum physics layer generates true random numbers, the algorithm layer adopts LWE lattice cryptography to resist quantum attacks, the system layer implements quantum key distribution through BB84 protocol, and the application layer dynamically optimizes the key refresh mechanism.
